frameworks.name: population-level encoding model (image reconstruction from fMRI patterns)
frameworks.one_line: Model-based reconstruction of remembered features from population activity in early visual cortex.

frameworks.name: top-down vs bottom-up signaling
frameworks.one_line: Frontoparietal top-down drive instantiates WM content in sensory cortex absent bottom-up input.

frameworks.name: sensory recruitment hypothesis of working memory
frameworks.one_line: WM content is held in feature-selective early sensory cortex during the delay.

core_question: How do population activity patterns in early visual cortex encode the content of working memory during a memory delay?
key_assumptions: Spatial population patterns in early visual cortex carry decodable WM content
Memory-guided saccade goals can be modeled as a reconstructable feature
Antisaccade dissociation isolates the goal from bottom-up visual stimulation
manipulations: Prosaccade vs antisaccade memory-guided procedure (stimulus dissociated from goal)
Time across the memory delay period
key_findings: Saccade goal locations were successfully reconstructed from early visual cortex delay-period activity
Reconstruction succeeded even when the visual stimulus was dissociated from the goal (antisaccade)
Visual-cortex representations remained stable but could also evolve from remembered stimulus to prospective goal
Frontal/parietal delay-period activity correlated trial-by-trial with reconstruction precision in visual cortex
interpretations: Early visual cortex represents WM content via top-down signals, presumably from frontal/parietal cortex
WM representations in sensory cortex are not purely retrospective stimulus traces — they can transform into prospective action goals
